does: This paper introduces generation exploitation attacks, a novel jailbreaking method that compromises aligned open-source LLMs by manipulating decoding parameters and sampling strategies rather than modifying input prompts. The authors demonstrate that by varying decoding hyperparameters (temperature, top-k, top-p) and sampling methods, they can increase misalignment rates from 0% to over 95% across eleven major model families including LLaMA2, Vicuna, Falcon, and MPT. Critically, this approach achieves superior attack success compared to existing adversarial prompt-based jailbreaks while requiring 30 times less computational cost, revealing a fundamental vulnerability in how alignment is implemented during model inference rather than through training or fine-tuning alone.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on adversarial attacks against aligned language models, building on prior research showing that careful alignment efforts can be circumvented by adversarial inputs. Previous jailbreak research focused primarily on crafting sophisticated text prompts or prompt injection techniques to trigger unsafe behaviors. This paper identifies a complementary attack vector by recognizing that alignment mechanisms may be brittle with respect to inference-time generation parameters. The research connects to broader work on the robustness of RLHF-aligned models and the distinction between training-time alignment and inference-time behavior, responding to the observation that defensive measures often address one attack surface while leaving others exposed.
enables: This work opens new research directions in understanding the mechanisms of LLM alignment and the role of generation strategies in maintaining safe behavior. It points toward the need for alignment methods that are robust to decoding parameter variations, potentially requiring training techniques or inference-time safeguards that constrain generation strategies. The findings enable future work on adversarial robustness evaluation protocols that test models across diverse decoding configurations rather than assuming fixed sampling strategies. Additionally, this research motivates defensive approaches such as parameter constraints, decoding verification schemes, or alignment training that explicitly accounts for generation diversity, advancing the field toward more fundamentally robust language models.
